trayHtml.en:
EUSD uses PowerSchool to share grades, attendance, and report cards with families.

What you can see

  • Current grades and assignment scores
  • Attendance records (absences, tardies)
  • Report cards and progress reports
  • Teacher comments

How to log in

  1. Go to the PowerSchool parent page on eusd.org
  2. Click the PowerSchool login link
  3. Enter your parent username and password

Don't have an account? Contact your child's school office — they'll create your parent account and give you login credentials.

Forgot your password? Use the "Forgot Password" link on the login screen, or call your school office for a reset.

Full details on eusd.org →

View all important dates for the EUSD school year — holidays, breaks, minimum days, and more. The full interactive calendar is in the Calendar section below.

Quick reference — 2025-2026

  • First day of school: August 19, 2025
  • Thanksgiving break: November 24–28, 2025
  • Winter break: December 22, 2025 – January 5, 2026
  • Spring break: April 2–13, 2026
  • Last day of school: June 10, 2026 (minimum day)

Download the printable calendar PDF or scroll down to the full calendar section for all dates and filter options.

Full details on eusd.org →

EUSD provides free breakfast and lunch to all students — no application or paperwork needed. The district participates in the Community Eligibility Provision (CEP), so every child eats free regardless of family income.

View menus

Daily menus are posted on EZSchoolLunch, where you can see what's being served, check nutrition info, and view allergen details.

Allergens & dietary needs

Menu items list common allergens. If your child has a specific food allergy or dietary restriction, contact your school's cafeteria manager or the Nutrition Services office.

Full details on eusd.org →

Bell schedules vary by school. Select your school in the "Find your school" section above to see its specific start and end times.

General schedule

  • Elementary schools: Most start between 7:45–8:30 AM and end between 2:15–3:00 PM
  • Middle schools: Most start between 7:30–8:00 AM and end between 2:30–3:00 PM

Minimum days

On minimum days, students are released early — usually around 12:15–12:45 PM for elementary and 12:30–1:00 PM for middle school. Check the school calendar for minimum day dates.

For your school's exact times, tap your school above or visit the bell schedules page.

Full details on eusd.org →

If your child will be absent or late, please notify the school as soon as possible — ideally before the school day starts.

How to report an absence

  1. Call your school's attendance line — find the phone number by tapping your school in the "Find your school" section above
  2. Online: Visit the EUSD attendance page for reporting options

What to include

  • Your child's full name and grade
  • Date(s) of absence
  • Reason for absence (illness, appointment, family emergency, etc.)
  • Your name and relationship to the student

Good to know

  • California law requires parents to clear absences within a specific timeframe
  • Uncleared absences are marked as unexcused (truant)
  • If your child will miss 3+ days, contact the school to arrange homework pickup

Full details on eusd.org →

Rooms (powered by Thrillshare) is EUSD's official app for school news, district announcements, and push notifications. It's free and available for iPhone and Android.

Why you need it

  • Get instant push notifications from your child's school
  • See news stories, photos, and event announcements
  • Receive emergency alerts and weather closures
  • Follow multiple schools if you have children at different sites

How to get started

  1. Download the Rooms app from the App Store or Google Play
  2. Search for "Escondido Union School District"
  3. Follow your child's school (and the district) to get notifications

You can also access the web version at escondidousdca.id.thrillshare.com.

Full details on eusd.org →

The best way to reach your child's teacher depends on your school. Here are your options:

Option 1: School website staff directory

Every school has a staff directory on its website. Find your school in the "Find your school" section above, then tap "Staff directory" in the school tray.

Option 2: Email

Most EUSD teacher email addresses follow the format: firstname.lastname@eusd.org. If you know the teacher's name, try that pattern.

Option 3: Call the school office

The school office can connect you to a teacher or pass along a message. Find your school's phone number in the "Find your school" section above.

Tips for a quick response

  • Include your child's full name, grade, and class period (for middle school)
  • Teachers typically respond within 1–2 school days
  • For urgent matters, call the school office directly

Full details on eusd.org →

Need to reach a school office? Select your school in the "Find your school" section above to see its phone number, address, and principal.

EUSD District Office

  • Phone: 760.432.2400
  • Address: 2310 Aldergrove Avenue, Escondido, CA 92029
  • Hours: Monday–Friday, 7:30 AM – 4:30 PM

Common departments

  • Enrollment: 760.432.2400
  • Transportation: 760.432.2400
  • Nutrition Services: 760.432.2400
  • Special Education: 760.432.2400

Full details on eusd.org →

Step 1: Find your school

Use the School Site Locator to find which school your child is assigned to based on your home address.

Step 2: Gather your documents

You'll need:

  • Proof of age — birth certificate, passport, or baptismal record
  • Proof of address — utility bill, lease/rental agreement, or mortgage statement (must be current)
  • Immunization records — California requires up-to-date immunizations for school entry
  • Previous school records (if transferring) — last report card or transcript

Step 3: Register online

Complete the online registration on the EUSD enrollment page. You'll then visit your assigned school to finalize enrollment.

Key dates

  • Kindergarten: Children must turn 5 by September 1 of the enrollment year
  • Transitional Kindergarten (TK): Children who turn 5 between September 2 and the following June 2
  • Open enrollment: Typically begins in January for the following school year

Full details on eusd.org →

Find commonly needed EUSD forms and documents. Many forms can be completed online; some require printing and returning to your school office.

Common forms

  • Enrollment / registration packet
  • Emergency contact update
  • Media release / photo permission
  • Interdistrict transfer request
  • Intradistrict transfer request
  • Medication authorization
  • Independent study contract

Contact your school office if you need a form that's not listed here, or if you need help completing any form.

Full details on eusd.org →

If you need to transfer your child to a different school within EUSD, here's what you need to know:

Intradistrict transfer (within EUSD)

If you want your child to attend a different EUSD school than the one assigned by your address:

  1. Complete an Intradistrict Transfer Request form (available at your current school office or online)
  2. Submit to the district office
  3. Transfers are approved based on available space at the requested school
  4. Open enrollment window is typically in January–February for the following year

Interdistrict transfer (from/to another district)

If you're moving into or out of EUSD:

  1. Contact the enrollment office at 760.432.2400
  2. Complete the Interdistrict Transfer form
  3. Both districts must approve the transfer

Moving within EUSD boundaries

If you're moving to a new address within EUSD, use the School Site Locator to check if your new address is in a different school boundary. If so, contact your school office to discuss your options.

Full details on eusd.org →

EXPLORE is EUSD's expanded learning program (formerly called ELOP). It offers free before-school and after-school care for students at participating sites.

What's included

  • Homework help and academic support
  • Enrichment activities (arts, STEM, sports, and more)
  • Before-school supervision (at participating schools)
  • After-school programs until 6:00 PM
  • Intersession and summer camps

Who can participate

The program is open to EUSD students. Priority is given to students who qualify for free or reduced-price meals, foster youth, English learners, and other priority groups. Availability varies by school site.

How to sign up

  1. Check with your school office to see if EXPLORE is available at your school
  2. Complete a registration form (available from your school or the Expanded Learning office)
  3. Space may be limited — sign up early

Full details on eusd.org →

If your child is struggling academically, there are several resources available through EUSD.

Start with the teacher

Your child's teacher is the best first contact. They can tell you:

  • Where your child stands relative to grade-level expectations
  • Specific areas that need improvement
  • What interventions or support the school is already providing

School-based support

  • Intervention programs: Schools offer reading and math intervention for students performing below grade level
  • English Learner (EL) support: Additional language support for students learning English
  • Tutoring: Some schools and community partners offer after-school tutoring — ask your school office

If you're still concerned

You can request a Student Study Team (SST) meeting. This brings together the teacher, administrator, and support staff to create a plan for your child.

If you suspect your child may have a learning disability, see the "Special education" section for information about evaluations and IEPs.

Full details on eusd.org →

EUSD supports the whole child — including physical health, mental health, and basic needs.

School counseling

Every school has access to a school counselor who can help with:

  • Social and emotional concerns
  • Behavioral support
  • Grief, anxiety, or family changes
  • Conflict resolution and peer issues

Ask your school office to connect you with the counselor.

Nutrition

All EUSD students receive free breakfast and lunch every school day through the Community Eligibility Provision — no application needed.

Project SUCCESS

Project SUCCESS provides extra support for students and families who are:

  • Military-connected families
  • Foster youth
  • Experiencing homelessness or housing insecurity

The program connects families with resources including school supplies, tutoring, counseling, and community services. Contact your school office or the EUSD Student Services department to learn more.

Full details on eusd.org →

If you believe your child may have a disability that affects their learning, EUSD offers support through special education services.

What are IEPs and 504 Plans?

  • IEP (Individualized Education Program): A detailed plan for students with identified disabilities who need specialized instruction. Includes specific goals, services (speech therapy, occupational therapy, etc.), and accommodations.
  • 504 Plan: Provides accommodations for students with a disability that affects a major life activity (like learning) but who don't need specialized instruction. Examples: extra time on tests, preferential seating, breaks.

How to get started

  1. Talk to your child's teacher first — share your concerns and ask what they're observing in the classroom
  2. Request an evaluation — you can request in writing that the school assess your child for special education. The school has 15 days to respond with an assessment plan.
  3. Assessment: If you consent, the school team will evaluate your child (this takes up to 60 days)
  4. IEP meeting: If your child qualifies, a team (including you) will develop the IEP together

Your rights

As a parent, you are an equal member of the IEP team. You have the right to participate in all meetings, review records, and disagree with decisions. The school must provide you with a copy of your parental rights.

Full details on eusd.org →

If you or your child knows about a potential threat to safety — whether it involves a student, a school, or the community — you can report it anonymously.

Say Something Anonymous Reporting

Say Something is a program by Sandy Hook Promise that lets anyone submit an anonymous tip about:

  • Threats of violence (to self or others)
  • Bullying or cyberbullying
  • Someone who may be struggling or in crisis
  • Weapons or dangerous situations
  • Drug or alcohol concerns

Tips are reviewed by trained crisis counselors 24/7 and forwarded to local authorities when needed.

How to report

  • Online: Visit the Say Something website
  • App: Download the Say Something app
  • Phone: Call or text the crisis line

In an emergency, always call 911 first.

Full details on sandyhookpromise.org →

If you have a concern about a district program, policy, or practice, EUSD has a formal process for addressing complaints.

What the Uniform Complaint Procedure covers

  • Discrimination or harassment (based on race, sex, disability, etc.)
  • Violations of state or federal education laws
  • Concerns about specific programs (special education, English learner programs, etc.)
  • Unsafe school conditions
  • Complaints about school fees or charges

How to file

  1. Start by talking to your child's teacher or school principal — many concerns can be resolved informally
  2. If the issue isn't resolved, submit a formal complaint in writing to the EUSD district office
  3. The district will investigate and respond within 60 days

You can also file a complaint directly with the California Department of Education if you believe the district hasn't addressed your concern.

Full details on eusd.org →

Which school is my child assigned to?

Your child's school is determined by your home address. Use the School Site Locator — enter your address and it will tell you exactly which elementary and middle school serve your neighborhood.

How do I enroll?

Visit the enrollment page on eusd.org to start the online registration process. After completing the online form, you'll visit your assigned school to finalize enrollment with your documents.

What documents do I need?

  • Proof of age: Birth certificate, passport, or baptismal record
  • Proof of address: Current utility bill, lease agreement, or mortgage statement
  • Immunization records: Up-to-date California immunizations
  • Previous school records (if transferring from another school)

Is lunch free?

Yes! EUSD participates in the Community Eligibility Provision (CEP), which means every student gets free breakfast and lunch — no application, no paperwork, no income forms. Your child eats free every day, no matter what.

What apps do I need?

Download these two apps to stay connected:

  1. Rooms (by Thrillshare) — for school news, announcements, and push notifications. Available on App Store and Google Play.
  2. PowerSchool — for checking grades and attendance. Your school office will give you login credentials.

What's the school day look like?

It depends on your school. Most elementary schools run from about 7:45–8:30 AM to 2:15–3:00 PM. Middle schools typically run 7:30–8:00 AM to 2:30–3:00 PM. Check the "Bell schedules" section or find your school above for exact times. On minimum days, students are dismissed early (usually around 12:15–1:00 PM).

Does EUSD have a dual language program?

Yes! EUSD offers a Dual Language Immersion program (Spanish/English) at four schools:

  • Farr Elementary
  • Glen View Elementary
  • Lincoln Elementary
  • Pioneer Elementary

The program uses a 90/10 model — in kindergarten, 90% of instruction is in Spanish and 10% in English, gradually shifting to 50/50 by upper elementary. It's designed for both native Spanish speakers and English speakers who want to become bilingual. Contact one of these schools directly to learn more about enrollment.

Full details on eusd.org →

Want to get involved at your child's school? EUSD welcomes parent volunteers!

How to volunteer

  1. Contact your school office — let them know you're interested in volunteering
  2. Complete a volunteer application — all volunteers must be approved by the district. This includes a background check (fingerprinting through the Department of Justice).
  3. TB clearance — California requires a TB risk assessment for school volunteers

Ways to help

  • Classroom support (reading with students, helping with projects)
  • Library volunteer
  • Field trip chaperone
  • School events and fundraisers
  • PTA/PTO (Parent-Teacher Association/Organization)
  • DELAC / DAC committees (District English Learner Advisory Committee / District Advisory Committee)

Contact your school office to learn about current volunteer needs and how to get started.

Full details on eusd.org →

The EUSD Board of Education holds regular public meetings where important decisions about the district are made. All meetings are open to the public.

Board meetings

  • Meetings are typically held on the 2nd and 4th Thursday of each month
  • Location: EUSD District Office, 2310 Aldergrove Avenue, Escondido
  • Agendas are posted online at least 72 hours before each meeting
  • Parents can attend and make public comments during designated time

What is LCAP?

The Local Control and Accountability Plan (LCAP) is the district's roadmap for how it will use funding to serve students. It outlines goals, actions, and spending priorities for a 3-year period.

The LCAP process includes public input sessions where parents can share their priorities. This is your chance to influence how the district spends its budget.

Get involved in governance

  • DAC (District Advisory Committee) — parent input on budget and programs
  • DELAC (District English Learner Advisory Committee) — focuses on English learner programs and services
  • PTA/PTO at your school — local parent organizations

Full details on eusd.org →
